title: Concurrency and Async
description: Questions about threads, async/await, and concurrent programming in Rust.

questions.question: How does Rust prevent data races at compile time?
questions.answer:
Rust prevents data races through the ownership and borrowing system combined with the Send and Sync traits.

A data race requires three conditions: (1) two or more threads access the same memory, (2) at least one is writing, and (3) no synchronization. Rust makes condition 2+3 impossible:

- The borrow checker enforces 'one writer XOR multiple readers' even across threads.
- Send trait: a type can be transferred to another thread. Non-Send types (like Rc) cannot be sent across thread boundaries.
- Sync trait: a type can be shared between threads via references. Non-Sync types (like Cell, RefCell) cannot be shared.

To share mutable state across threads, you must use synchronization primitives like Mutex<T> or atomic types, which are both Send and Sync. The compiler rejects any program that could have a data race.

questions.question: What is the difference between std::thread and tokio::spawn?
Explain the difference between Mutex and RwLock. When would you use each?
questions.answer:
Mutex (mutual exclusion): one thread at a time, whether reading or writing. Simple and efficient for short critical sections.

let data = Mutex::new(vec![1, 2, 3]);
let mut guard = data.lock().unwrap();
guard.push(4); // exclusive access

RwLock (read-write lock): allows multiple concurrent readers OR one exclusive writer. Better when reads greatly outnumber writes.

let data = RwLock::new(config);
let guard = data.read().unwrap();  // Multiple readers allowed
// OR
let mut guard = data.write().unwrap();  // Exclusive writer

Use Mutex when:
- Critical sections are short
- Read and write frequency is similar
- Simplicity is preferred

Use RwLock when:
- Many readers, few writers
- Read operations are slow (holding the lock longer)

Caveat: RwLock has higher overhead than Mutex. For quick operations, Mutex may be faster even with many readers.

questions.question: What does 'async fn returns a Future' mean? Why are Futures lazy in Rust?
questions.answer:
When you write async fn foo() -> String, the function does not actually return a String. It returns an impl Future<Output = String> -- a state machine that will produce a String when polled to completion.

Futures are lazy: calling an async function does nothing. It just creates the Future value. The computation only starts when you .await it (which polls the future).

This is different from JavaScript Promises (which start immediately) or Go goroutines (which run immediately).

Why lazy? It gives you control:
- You decide when to start the work
- You can compose futures before executing them
- No wasted work if you decide not to await
- The runtime can batch and schedule more efficiently

let future = fetch_data(); // Nothing happens yet
// ... can do other setup ...
let result = future.await; // NOW it runs

questions.question: What are Send and Sync? Give examples of types that are NOT Send or Sync.
questions.answer:
Send: a type can be moved (transferred) to another thread. If T: Send, you can pass it to std::thread::spawn or tokio::spawn.

Sync: a type can be shared between threads via references. If T: Sync, then &T is Send. Multiple threads can read the value simultaneously.

Relationship: T is Sync if and only if &T is Send.

Not Send:
- Rc<T>: reference count is not atomic, would cause data races
- Raw pointers (*const T, *mut T): no safety guarantees

Not Sync:
- Cell<T>, RefCell<T>: interior mutability without synchronization
- Rc<T>: not thread-safe

Both Send and Sync:
- Arc<T>: atomic reference counting
- Mutex<T>: synchronizes access
- All primitive types (i32, bool, etc.)

The compiler auto-derives Send and Sync when all fields are Send/Sync. You manually implement them (unsafe impl) for custom synchronization types.

questions.question: How would you share state between multiple async tasks?
What is tokio::task::spawn_blocking and when do you need it?
questions.answer:
spawn_blocking runs a synchronous (blocking) closure on a dedicated thread pool, separate from tokio's async worker threads.

You need it when:
- Calling synchronous I/O (std::fs, blocking database drivers)
- Running CPU-intensive computations
- Using libraries that block (e.g., some C FFI calls)

let result = tokio::task::spawn_blocking(|| {
    std::thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(5)); // OK here!
    expensive_computation()
}).await.unwrap();

Why it matters: tokio's async threads use cooperative scheduling. If you block an async thread (with CPU work or blocking I/O), ALL tasks on that thread stall. spawn_blocking moves the work off the async threads.

Rule: never do blocking work directly in an async fn. Either use async I/O (tokio::fs, async database drivers) or spawn_blocking for synchronous operations.
